<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And then there was the season after Pentecost, what we call ordinary time. The vestments are green and we hear a lot of Jesus's parables. It is a season that can fill at once adrift and rudderless.<|18.50|><|endoftext|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We are now three Sundays into Advent, the season that precedes Christmas, that great feast of the incarnation in which God takes on flesh and moves into our neighborhood, as one translation says.<|18.56|><|endoftext|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But before God does that, we wait in anticipation of the gift that is Jesus Christ.<|8.62|>
<|9.44|> We wait for his birth at Christmas and for his coming again in glory to judge the living and the dead.<|16.78|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Just like that, it feels as if the church calendar is back in sync with our collective COVID-imposed calendar.<|11.84|>

<|13.78|> Every day, I walk within a block of a Trader Joe's, and every day for the past nine months, almost without fail, there has been a line of shoppers waiting on the sidewalk to enter the store.<|28.30|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> They are waiting in order to keep one another safe.<|6.10|>
<|6.10|> Because as we have learned over the past nine months,<|10.30|>
<|10.30|> this virus thrives on crowds in close quarters.<|16.30|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Every day, I walk past an urgent care clinic that doubles as a COVID testing site where cars are neatly parked one next to the other with patients and<|17.70|><|endoftext|>

<|6.38|> after which they depart that parking lot and return home to await their results.<|14.78|>
<|14.78|> I pray for them when I walk by because I do not know what series of events and<|21.92|>
<|21.92|> social contacts that led them to their test.<|26.90|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> They are waiting to hear not detected or positive and what those two very different test results imply for one's life in their immediate wakes.<|15.32|><|endoftext|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> On alternating Sundays, I stand just a few steps away from this very spot in either administer Holy Communion to a steady flow of people drawn here by Jesus himself,<|13.36|>
<|14.28|> who stand in a line or sit in a line just outside these doors,<|21.90|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and wait for their turn, or I stand outside and greet people as they come to stand in line.<|9.64|>
<|9.64|> And there are many more of you at home who are wisely and safely waiting to come here<|17.94|>
<|17.94|> until a vaccine is widely distributed and herd immunity is successfully reached.<|24.90|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Waiting. Until.<|3.84|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|><|endoftext|>

<|16.88|> I hope she told them that growing a person inside one's body does not renew strength, it saps energy.<|28.10|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> "We can be the same way in our spiritual lives," Dr. Coleman goes on to say.<|6.44|>
